title: Film Loading and Storage Protection
narrationScript: Everything in this lesson happens before the first photon of the exposure, and yet it decides whether your radiograph is clean or covered in artifacts. Unprocessed film remembers everything that happens to it. Bend it, slide it, shock it, warm it, or let a stray gamma photon through the wall of its bin, and the film files the insult away and prints it on the finished radiograph, usually somewhere inconvenient, sometimes looking exactly like a defect. So we start with the general rules for handling. Wash your hands and dry them completely, because moisture, skin oils, and traces of chemistry transfer to the emulsion as fingerprints and smudges. Handle sheet film only by its edges and corners. Never grab the middle, never drag a sheet across the bench, and never let two sheets slide against each other, because friction scratches the soft emulsion and builds static. Static is a real hazard in dry climates and dry winter shops. Pull film quickly out of a box or a holder and you can hear the crackle, and each little discharge exposes the emulsion, printing black branching marks like lightning or trees, or clusters of black dots. Move slowly and deliberately, keep the room humidity in the working range, and avoid synthetic sleeves that build charge. Bending is the other classic sin. Kink a film and you get a crescent shaped crimp mark, and the timing tells the story, because a film kinked before exposure shows a light crescent, while a film kinked after exposure shows a dark one. Take off rings and watches, keep the bench bare except for what you are loading, and work under a proper safelight only. Film arrives in several kinds of packaging, and each is handled differently. Bulk boxed sheet film comes in sealed inner bags with paper interleaving between the sheets, and it must be opened and loaded only in the darkroom. Factory sealed packets hold a single sheet, sometimes with thin lead screens built in, inside a light tight envelope, so you can carry them into daylight, shoot them, and open them back in the darkroom for processing. Roll film comes in long light tight rolls in widths from about seventy millimeters up to seventeen inches, and it exists mainly for work like girth welds, where a strip wrapped clear around a pipe records the whole circumference in one panoramic exposure. Loading a cassette or holder is a small ritual worth doing the same way every time. Inspect the cassette for worn edges, torn felt, and bad hinges, because a cassette that leaks light is scrap until it is repaired. Inspect and clean the lead screens, since every scratch, dent, bit of lint, or flake of dried chemistry on a screen sits in intimate contact with the film and will print as a false indication. Then, under safelight, lift one sheet by the edges, lay it between the front and back screens, and close the holder gently so the film cannot shift and the screens press into firm, even contact. The standard sandwich, from the source side in, runs front screen about five thousandths of an inch of lead, then the film, then the back screen about ten thousandths, often with extra backing lead behind everything to swallow backscatter. For roll work you measure and cut the length in the darkroom, sandwich the strip in screen material, wrap it light tight, and tape it so nothing shifts as it bends around the pipe. Avoid kinks, because every kink is a crimp mark waiting to appear. Finally, storage. Film ages even sitting still, and heat, humidity, radiation, fumes, and pressure all speed it up. Store boxes on edge, standing like books, never in flat stacks where the weight makes pressure marks. Keep the stock room cool and dry, roughly fifty to seventy five degrees Fahrenheit and thirty to sixty percent relative humidity, away from steam lines and radiators. Keep film away from radiation, which means well separated from exposure bays and source storage, with the day's working film in a shielded bin, because film integrates dose, and a little fog every day adds up to a gray, flat film. Keep it away from ammonia, solvents, and other active fumes. Rotate stock, first in, first out, and respect expiry dates, because age raises base plus fog and eats contrast. And if film is stored cold, let the sealed package warm to room temperature before opening it, or moisture will condense straight onto the emulsion. Protect the film now, and the radiograph will have nothing to confess later.
imagePrompt: Close-up of a technician's clean bare hands lifting a sheet of radiographic film by its corners above an open film holder with lead screens on a darkroom loading bench lit by deep red safelight.

sections.heading: General Rules for Handling Unprocessed Film
sections.body:
Unprocessed film remembers every insult - bend it, slide it, touch it with damp fingers, and the result prints on the finished radiograph, often looking enough like a discontinuity to cause real trouble. The general rules exist to keep the record clean.

- **Clean, dry hands.** Wash and dry thoroughly; skin oils, moisture, and traces of chemistry become fingerprints and smudges. Never handle film after touching fixer or developer without washing first.
- **Edges and corners only.** Never grip the face of a sheet, and never drag film across a bench or another sheet - friction scratches the soft emulsion.
- **Move deliberately.** Pulling film fast from a box or holder in dry air generates static discharges that expose the emulsion: jagged black tree- and branch-shaped marks, or clusters of black dots. Keep humidity in the working range and slow down.
- **No bending.** A sharp kink leaves a crescent-shaped crimp mark that can mimic a discontinuity.
- **Bare bench.** Rings, watches, and anything abrasive come off; only film, screens, and holders share the surface.
- **Safelight only**, with boxes and bins closed again as soon as a sheet is withdrawn.

sections.title: Crimp marks tell on you - and tell time
sections.body: A crescent-shaped mark from kinking film is a **crimp mark**, and its tone testifies to when the damage happened: kinked **before** exposure it images **light**; kinked **after** exposure it images **dark**. Either way it can mimic or mask a real indication. Handle sheets by the edges with two hands and crimp marks simply do not occur.

sections.heading: Types of Film Packaging
sections.body:
- **Bulk boxed sheet film** - standard sizes (such as 4.5 x 17 in. and 14 x 17 in.) sealed in a moisture-proof inner bag, sheets separated by interleaving paper. It is opened, loaded, and unloaded only under safelight, and the working stock lives in the light-tight bin.
- **Factory-sealed packets (envelope film)** - one sheet per light-tight envelope, often with thin lead screens packed in contact with the film. The sealed packet can be carried, positioned, and exposed in daylight; it is opened only back in the darkroom for processing. Respect the marked source side, and protect packets from pressure - the envelope does not stop a careless thumb or a dropped wrench.
- **Roll film** - long light-tight rolls in widths from about 70 mm up to 17 in., made for jobs like circumferential girth-weld panoramics, where a strip wraps the full pipe joint for a single centered exposure, and for cut-to-length economy in production darkrooms.
- **Pre-loaded and vacuum-packed holders** - factory film-screen sandwiches with the air evacuated so screen contact is essentially perfect; convenient and consistent, at a higher price per sheet.

sections.heading: Loading Sheet Film: Cassettes and Holders
sections.body:
Loading rewards ritual - the same steps, in the same order, every time.

First inspect the **cassette or flexible holder**: torn edge seams, worn felt light seals, and sprung hinges or closures mean light leaks, and a leaking holder is scrap until repaired. Then inspect and clean the **lead screens** with an approved cleaner and lint-free wipes. Every scratch, dent, crease, flake of dried chemistry, or piece of lint sits in intimate contact with the film during exposure and prints as a false indication; deeply scratched or dented screens get retired, not excused.

Under safelight, lift one sheet by its edges, center it between the front and back screens, and close the holder so the film cannot shift and the screens bear evenly - **film-screen contact** is part of image sharpness. The standard industrial sandwich, source side in: front lead screen about **0.005 in.**, the double-emulsion film, back lead screen about **0.010 in.**, and, where backscatter threatens, additional backing lead behind the stack. Load with the bench bare, hands dry, and the film bin closed before the white light ever comes back on.

sections.caption: Cross-section of a loaded film holder: front lead screen, double-emulsion film, thicker back screen, and backing lead, all in tight contact between the holder faces.

sections.heading: Loading Roll Film
sections.body:
Roll film turns the darkroom into a small production line. Mount the roll in its **light-tight dispenser**, draw off the measured length for the joint, and cut it square - in total darkness or under safelight, per the film's instructions. Sandwich the strip between lead screen strips or feed it into flexible vinyl holders, then wrap or seal the assembly light-tight and tape it so nothing shifts as the strip bends around the pipe.

Two cautions rule the work. **No kinks**: every sharp bend is a crimp mark waiting to develop, and long strips kink easily, so roll and carry them in generous loops. **Mark orientation**: identify the source side and the ends so the crew positions the strip correctly against the weld and your location markers read true. Handle finished belts like the film they are - by the edges, away from pressure, in clean wrapping - and process promptly after exposure rather than letting exposed strips age in a hot truck.

sections.heading: Protecting Film in Storage
sections.body:
Film ages on the shelf, and heat, humidity, radiation, fumes, and pressure all accelerate it. Aging shows as rising **base-plus-fog** and falling speed and contrast - many codes cap film-system fog around **0.30 density**, and stock that cannot meet it has no business on code work.

- **Cool and dry**: roughly **50-75 °F** and **30-60% relative humidity**, away from steam lines, radiators, and damp floors. Original sealed packaging is moisture-proof - until opened. Cold-stored film must warm to room temperature before the seal is broken, or moisture condenses directly onto the emulsion.
- **On edge**: store boxes standing like books, never in flat stacks - the weight of stacked boxes presses pressure marks into sheet after sheet.
- **Away from radiation**: film integrates dose continuously, so keep stock well away from exposure bays and source storage, and keep the working supply in a shielded, lead-lined bin. A little daily fog accumulates into a gray, low-contrast film.
- **Away from active fumes**: ammonia, hydrogen sulfide, formaldehyde, strong solvents, and processing chemistry vapors all fog or stain emulsions.
- **Rotate stock**: first in, first out, with expiry dates checked at receipt and again at use.

sections.caption: Storage rules at a glance: boxes on edge, cool dry conditions, shielding from radiation, no chemical fumes, and first-in first-out rotation.

checkQuestions.question: The correct way to pick up a sheet of unprocessed film is:
options: With clean, dry hands, by the edges or corners, without sliding it across surfaces
With clean gloves, gripping the center so the edges stay pristine
Quickly, to minimize total safelight exposure time
By sliding it off the stack onto the bench to avoid finger contact
checkQuestions.explanation: Edges and corners with clean dry hands avoid fingerprints, scratches, and pressure marks; fast pulls and sliding generate static and abrasion artifacts.

checkQuestions.question: A radiograph shows a dark, crescent-shaped mark near the weld. Among handling causes, this most likely means the film was:
options: Kinked or bent sharply after exposure
Kinked or bent sharply before exposure
Exposed to safelight too long
Stored under a heavy stack of boxes
checkQuestions.explanation: Crimp marks are crescent-shaped: dark when the film was bent after exposure, light when bent before exposure. Safelight fog and stack pressure produce different artifact patterns.

checkQuestions.question: Which film packaging is the natural choice for a single panoramic exposure of a complete pipe girth weld?
options: Bulk boxed 14 x 17 in. sheet film
Roll film cut to wrap the full circumference
A factory-sealed single-sheet packet
A vacuum-packed rigid cassette
checkQuestions.explanation: Roll film comes in long lengths and flexible widths so a single strip can band the entire joint and record the whole circumference in one centered exposure.

checkQuestions.question: In a typical industrial film load, the lead screens are arranged with:
options: About 0.005 in. lead in front of the film and about 0.010 in. behind it
About 0.010 in. lead in front of the film and about 0.005 in. behind it
Equal 0.020 in. screens on both sides
A fluorescent screen in front and lead foil behind
checkQuestions.explanation: The thinner (about 0.005 in.) screen faces the source so it intensifies without excessive filtration; the thicker (about 0.010 in.) back screen adds intensification and helps absorb backscatter.

checkQuestions.question: Which storage practice set protects unexposed film best?
options: Boxes on edge, 50-75 °F, 30-60% RH, shielded from radiation, oldest stock used first
Boxes stacked flat to save space, room temperature, used in whatever order is handy
Boxes on edge next to the exposure bay door for convenience, any humidity
Refrigerated boxes opened immediately on removal so they stay cold
checkQuestions.explanation: On-edge storage prevents pressure marks, the cool dry range slows age fog, shielding prevents radiation fog, and first-in first-out keeps stock inside its expiry dates.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality 2.2-2.2.3 (film loading and handling)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality 2.3 (protection of film in storage)
ASTM E1254-style guidance on storage of radiographs and unprocessed film (reference practice)
Film manufacturer handling and storage instructions (reference practice)
